metadata.seed: I hate looking at myself in the mirror, every time I do I feel like the ugliest person in the whole world. I feel like nobody will ever love me.

patient.query: I hate looking at myself in the mirror, every time I do I feel like the ugliest person in the whole world. I feel like nobody will ever love me.

automatic_thoughts: I am the ugliest person in the whole world

neutral: Does not recognize self in mirror (finding)
Own reflection in mirror seems unreal (finding)

llm_response.response:
REASONING:
"retrieved_concepts_used": ["triggers", "automatic_thoughts", "emotions", "behaviors", "ENTAILMENT"]

FINAL RESPONSE:
It sounds incredibly painful to experience such strong negative feelings when you look in the mirror. Those thoughts – that you’re the ugliest person and unlovable – must be very distressing. Let’s explore what’s happening when you have these thoughts and feelings.

patient.query: Okay, I’ll do my best. I guess I just feel really anxious about how I look all the time. It’s like I can’t shake off the feeling that people are judging me, and it makes me want to hide away. Sometimes, I don't even want to go out because I’m so worried about what others might think.

automatic_thoughts: I can't shake off this feeling
I don't want to go out

llm_response.response: It sounds like this anxiety about your appearance is significantly impacting your desire to go out and connect with others. What specifically makes you feel like people are judging you? Let’s explore what’s contributing to this feeling and see if we can understand it better.

patient.query: Yeah, I suppose so. It’s like I just get this overwhelming sense that everyone’s looking at me, and they can see all my flaws. Even in casual situations, like at work or when I’m out with friends, I can't help but feel like I’m being scrutinized. It's exhausting, really. Sometimes I wish I could just blend in and not be noticed at all.

automatic_thoughts: they can see all my flaws

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly draining to feel like you're constantly under observation and judgment. That sense of being scrutinized can certainly lead to exhaustion, and wanting to disappear is a natural response to feeling that way. Let’s explore what’s contributing to this feeling and how it impacts you.

patient.query: Yeah, it really is draining. I think part of it comes from when I was younger—people would comment on my looks a lot, you know? I just remember feeling like I had to meet this standard that was impossible to reach. It feels like that pressure has stuck with me. It's like I can't shake off the fear of disappointing people or being seen as less than perfect.

automatic_thoughts: I have to meet an impossible standard
I will be seen as less than perfect

llm_response.response: It sounds like those comments from when you were younger have had a lasting impact, and it’s understandable that you feel drained by the pressure. It takes a lot of energy to constantly feel like you need to meet an impossible standard. Can you tell me more about what it’s like to feel that pressure now?

patient.query: Yeah, it just feels heavy all the time. Like, I’m always on guard, trying to present myself in a certain way. If I see someone looking at me, my mind races with all these thoughts—what are they thinking? Did I do something wrong? It can ruin my whole day if I think I didn’t look right or if someone made a comment. I guess it just makes me really self-conscious, and I end up overthinking everything.

behaviors: trying to present myself in a certain way

neutral: Anxiety about body function or health (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds exhausting to feel like you’re constantly monitoring yourself and trying to control how you’re perceived. What’s particularly difficult seems to be the rush of thoughts that follow when you feel someone’s attention on you. Let’s explore those thoughts a bit more – what specifically goes through your mind when you notice someone looking at you?

patient.query: Yeah, it really is. When I notice someone looking at me, it’s like a switch flips in my head. I start thinking about every little thing—like, is my hair okay? Are my clothes flattering? I worry that they’ll see something they don’t like and judge me for it. Sometimes, I even imagine them talking about me afterwards, like, “Did you see how they looked?” It spirals so quickly, and then I just freeze up. It makes it hard to

automatic_thoughts: they'll see something they don't like

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that rapid shift in your thoughts when you notice someone looking at you. It’s understandable that you’d feel frozen when your mind is racing with those kinds of worries. Let’s explore what’s behind those thoughts about how others perceive you.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it is distressing. It just feels like there’s this constant comparison happening in my head. I think it stems from wanting to fit in and be accepted, you know? But the more I try, the more anxious I become because I feel like I’m never quite enough. It’s like I’m always trying to gauge how well I measure up to everyone else, and sometimes it feels like I’m failing at that.

triggers: trying to gauge how well I measure up

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of pressure to measure up, and that’s understandably distressing. This constant comparison can be exhausting, and it’s natural to feel anxious when you’re striving for something that feels unattainable. Let’s explore what’s driving this need to compare yourself to others.

patient.query: Yeah, it definitely feels overwhelming. I guess I’ve always felt like I had to compete to be seen or valued, especially growing up. When others would point out what they liked or didn’t like about me, it sort of stuck. It’s like I feel this need to prove that I belong, and if I don’t look a certain way, I worry I won’t fit in. It’s hard to shake that feeling off, and it just adds to the pressure.

automatic_thoughts: I have to prove that I belong

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve carried a significant burden of feeling like you need to earn your place. It’s understandable that you’d feel overwhelmed when you’ve consistently felt the need to prove yourself. Can you tell me more about what it was like growing up and how those experiences shaped your beliefs about belonging?

patient.query: Yeah, I think those experiences really shaped how I see myself. Growing up, I always felt pressure to look a certain way or act a certain way to be accepted. There were times when I felt like the odd one out, and it was like I had to put on this mask just to fit in. If someone pointed out something I didn't like about myself, it would stick with me for a long time. I felt like I had to constantly adapt to what others wanted, and it made me

triggers: pressure to look a certain way
pressure to act a certain way
feeling like the odd one out
automatic_thoughts: I have to put on a mask to fit in
I need to adapt to what others want
I am not accepted as I am

neutral: Enduring personality change after catastrophic experience (finding)
Enduring personality change after psychiatric illness (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like those experiences created a strong sense of needing to adjust yourself to gain acceptance. It's understandable that you feel your self-image has been shaped by those pressures. Let’s explore what it’s like to feel like you’re putting on a mask and how that impacts you.

patient.query: Yeah, it really feels like I’m wearing a mask sometimes. I try so hard to present this version of myself that I think people will accept, but it’s exhausting. I mean, there are days when I don’t even recognize the person I’m pretending to be. And when I finally take the mask off, it’s like this wave of relief mixed with fear, because I worry that the real me won’t be good enough. It makes it hard to connect with people genuinely.

automatic_thoughts: people won't accept the real me
the real me won't be good enough

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're experiencing a real struggle with feeling authentic and connected. That sense of exhaustion from constantly presenting a certain image must be incredibly draining. Let’s explore what’s behind that relief and fear you feel when you consider letting go of that presentation.
